title: NA-ULIP
word_text:
GST in India: Meaning, Types & Structure
The taxation system is often perceived as a complex puzzle. There are various taxes levied on different stages of production that can often make it difficult to decode. However, with the coming of GST, the taxation system has become easier. GST is the goods and services tax, is a single tax system that has replaced erstwhile various indirect taxes bringing uniformity. Let us understand all about GST - its types, implementation process, latest reforms, benefits and drawbacks.
What is GST (Goods and Services Tax) in India?
The meaning of GST is as simple as the tax system can get. Goods and services tax is the indirect tax that has replaced various erstwhile indirect taxes in India. Earlier, there were VAT, service tax, excise duty, etc. Now, all these are covered under a single tax, GST. It can also be seen as a multi-stage, comprehensive, and destination-based tax. Goods and services tax is benefitting both taxpayers and the government in multiple ways. The shortcomings of multiple indirect taxes have also been reduced to a great extent with introduction of GST. To summarise all about GST, it’s the biggest tax reform that India has seen to make the whole taxation process simple and transparent.
Note: GST is a destination-based tax. That means the state where goods are being consumed will receive the tax and not the state that manufactures the goods. 2
History of GST
In India, the GST Law came into force on 1st July 2017.1 There has been numerous reforms in GST after introduction, with the latest one in 2025.
Types of GST in India
Depending on the transaction of goods & services and the tax collecting authority, there are 4 types of GST:
● CGST
Central Goods and Services Tax. The central government governs the CGST. It is collected on supply happening within a state.
● SGST
State Goods and Services Tax. The state government is responsible for the collection and governance of SGST. The tax is levied for a supply within the state.
● UTGST
Union Territory Goods and Services Tax. Union territories are the governing authorities for UTGST. It works similarly to SGST.
● IGST
Integrated Goods and Services Tax. The centre collects IGST, which is later split between the state(s) or UT(s) and the central government, as it is levied on supply happening between 2 or more states.
Given below is a table to explain the broad working of GST:
This example will give further clarity on tax rules:
Suppose goods worth ₹1,00,000 is sold from West Bengal to Assam. As per the GST law, IGST at 18% i.e. ₹18,000 will be charged.
If the goods worth ₹1,00,000 are sold within West Bengal at 18% GST, the tax will split into 2 parts – Centre receives ₹9,000 as CGST and West Bengal state receives 9,000 as SGST.
How are taxes implemented and collected?
Broadly, two types of purchase and sale happen. Taxes are levied based on the nature of the purchase and sale of goods and services. To understand GST meaning, let's have a look at how taxes are levied, collected, and shared:
● Intra-state supply
Goods and services that are produced and consumed within a state or union territory are referred to as intra-state transactions. As explained above, CGST and SGST/UTGST are levied on these transactions.
Post introduction of GST 2.0 reforms, now there are only 2 main tax slabs of 5% and 18%, with a special 40% on selected luxury and ‘sin’ items.
● Inter-state supply
On the other hand, goods and services that happen between two or more states are referred to as inter-state transactions. Only IGST is levied on these transactions. Exports and imports in India also attract IGST. The centre collects IGST, and according to the IGST Act, the tax is shared with the participating states and union territories.
GST tax structure for various goods & services
Effective from September 22, 2025, the government has simplified the GST structure from 4 tax slabs to 2 main tax slabs of 5% and 18% along with an exclusive 40% slab for luxury and sin consumables. A lot of products have been made tax-free or shifted to lower tax slabs to simplify the system and make life easier for common man. Here’s the latest tax sab as per GST 2.0:
● List of items with 0% GST
GST on individual life insurance products (term plan, ULIP, or endowment) and individual health insurance have been set as 0%. So, if you are wondering, is GST applicable on ULIPs, the answer is ‘No’ as per the latest changes.
Various basic edibles and health services charge no GST rate. Some of these include:
Fresh Milk
Salt
Eggs
Curd and lassi, other than pre-packaged and labelled
Gur, other than pre-packaged and labelled
Besan, etc.
New food items, stationaries and medicines to join this list are:
UHT milk
prepackaged or labelled chena or paneer
Indian breads like roti, pizza bread, paratha, chapati and khakhra
Erasers
Exercise book, graph book, & laboratory note book and notebooks
Pencil sharpeners, pencils, crayons, pastels, drawing charcoals and tailor’s chalk
Maps and globes
Uncoated paper and paperboard
30+ life-saving drugs and medicines
● List of items with 5% GST
There are multiple products and services that attract 5% GST post GST 2.0 reforms:
Daily items like hair oil, shampoo, talcum powder, face powder, toothpaste
Butter and other fats and oils derived from milk, including ghee; dairy spreads
Cheese, other than chena or paneer
Mixes and doughs for the preparation of bread, pastry and other baker's wares
Pastry, cakes, biscuits and other bakers’ wares, whether or not containing cocoa; communion wafers, empty cachets of a kind suitable for pharmaceutical use, sealing wafers, rice paper and similar products; Un-fried or un-cooked snack pellets, by whatever name called, manufactured through process of extrusion; Extruded or expanded products, savoury or salted
Vegetables, fruit, nuts and other edible parts of plants, prepared or preserved by vinegar or acetic acid
Namkeens, bhujia, mixture, chabena and similar edible preparations in ready for consumption form, whether or not pre-packaged and labelled
All goods, including sauces and preparations therefor, mixed condiments and mixed seasonings; mustard flour and meal and prepared mustard including Curry paste, mayonnaise and salad dressings
Chocolates and other food preparations containing cocoa
Tractors (except road tractors for semi-trailers of engine capacity more than 1800 cc)
Agricultural, horticultural or forestry machinery for soil preparation or cultivation; lawn or sports-ground rollers; Parts [8432 90]
Marble and travertine blocks
Granite crude or roughly trimmed
All Drugs and medicines including their salts and esters and diagnostic test kits; formulations manufactured from bulk drugs [other than those specified at nil at S. No. 113 of notification No. 10/2025-Central Tax (Rate) dated 17th September, 2025]
Instruments and appliances used in medical, surgical, dental or veterinary sciences, including scientigraphic apparatus, other electro medical apparatus and sight-testing instruments
Yarn of manmade staple fibres
Sulphuric acid, Nitric acid, Ammonia
Charger or charging station for Electrically operated vehicles
● List of items with 18% GST
18% GST is levied on multiple products. However, under GST 2.0, many items from here have been moved to lower slab of 5% and various products from 28% slab has now been put here. Some of the items covered here are:
Air-conditioning machines, comprising a motor-driven fan and elements for changing the temperature and humidity, including those machines in which the humidity cannot be separately regulated
Vacuum cleaners
Refrigerators, freezers and other refrigerating or freezing equipment, electric or other; heat pumps other than air conditioning machines of heading 8415
Motorcycles (including mopeds) and cycles fitted with an auxiliary motor not exceeding 350 cc, with or without side-cars; side-cars
Portland cement, aluminous cement, slag cement, super sulphate cement and similar hydraulic cements, whether or not coloured or in the form of clinkers
Motor vehicles for the transport of ten or more persons, including the driver [including buses for use in public transport, which exclusively run on Bio-fuels]
Motor vehicles cleared as ambulances duly fitted with all the fitments, furniture and accessories necessary for an ambulance from the factory manufacturing such motor vehicles
Fork-lift trucks; other works trucks fitted with lifting or handling equipment
Three wheeled vehicles
Articles of apparel and clothing accessories, knitted or crocheted, of sale value exceeding Rs. 2500 per piece
Coal; briquettes, ovoids and similar solid fuels manufactured from coal
Lignite, whether or not agglomerated, excluding jet
Peat (including peat litter), whether or not agglomerated
All dutiable articles intended for personal use
● List of items with 40% GST
The government has levied a special 40% tax on luxury products and some harmful products. The list includes:
All goods [including aerated waters], containing added sugar or other sweetening matter or flavoured
Other non-alcoholic beverages [other than those specified in Schedule I of this notification]
Caffeinated Beverages
Carbonated beverages of fruit drink or carbonated beverages with fruit juice
Motor vehicles with both spark-ignition internal combustion reciprocating piston engine and electric motor as motors for propulsion, of engine capacity exceeding 1200cc or of length exceeding 4000 mm (Explanation. - For the purposes of this entry, the specification of the motor vehicle shall be determined as per the Motor Vehicles Act, 1988 (59 of 1988) and the rules made there under.)
Motor cycles of engine capacity exceeding 350 cc
Aircraft for personal use
Yacht and other vessels for pleasure or sports
Revolvers and pistols, other than those of heading 9303 or 9304
Smoking pipes (including pipe bowls) and cigar or cigarette holders, and parts thereof
Specified actionable claim; (Explanation: ―specified actionable claim” as defined in section 2(102A) of the CGST Act, 2017 means the actionable claim involved in or by way of – (i) betting; (ii) casinos; (iii) gambling; (iv) horse racing; (v) lottery; or (vi) online money gaming)
Benefits of GST in India
The application of GST has been a history-changing reform in India to simplify the complex tax system and make it more transparent. Here are some of the advantages of implementing GST in India:
Simplified Tax Structure
After replacing various indirect taxes like VAT, service tax, excise etc. with a single tax structure throughout the nation, GST has simplified business dealings, and made tax filing easier.
Reduction of Tax Cascading
By allowing businesses to claim Input Tax Credit, GST reduces “tax on tax” thus lowering the product costs.
Better Transparency and Compliance
The online filing of tax and e-way bill introduction have made the entire tax filing system transparent
Consumer Savings Boost
With GST 2.0, many goods have been moved into lower or zero-tax brackets, cutting costs for households, especially on essentials.
Stimulates Demand and Growth
Lower taxes on daily use items and appliances will aid in increasing purchasing power, driving consumption and economic growth.
Revenue Boost and Ease of Business
The government revenue has increased with more businesses being covered under GST tax base and better compliance via the use of technology. Also, easy registration process, uniform rates, and simple filing have made it easier for businesses to work smoothly throughout the nation.
What are the Latest GST Reforms?
GST 2.0, taking effect from September 22, 2025 has simplified the Indian tax regime with 2 rate slabs of 5% as merit rate, 18% as standard rate, and 40% as a special de-merit rate on luxury and sin goods. Essential products like milk, roti, life-saving drugs have been exempted from tax, while bicycles, milk products have been put under 5% tax slab helping consumers to save more. GST on individual life insurance and individual health insurance has been completely removed. Some procedural changes to be expected are faster refunds, easier registration, MRP revision on unsold stocks, and better compliance via technology. These cover all about GST’s next -generation transformation.
GST vs Earlier Tax System
Before GST, India had a complex tax structure comprising of multiple tax rules like VAT, Excise, Service tax, and CST, which led to cascading of taxes and tax evasions. After GST, a unified system has been implemented which uses input tax credit to abolish cascading tax, reducing costs, and bringing more transparency. Unlike the fragmented old regime, GST is destination-based which means that tax is imposed where goods and services are finally consumed. The dual model (CGST/SGST for intra-state and IGST for inter-state) ensures that revenue is shared uniformly between the central and state governments.
Challenges of GST in India
Some of the common challenges with GST in India are as follows –
There are different tax slab rates for different goods and services. This can pose a problem for businesses to know and comply with the right tax rates.
Technology plays an important part in GST implementation. As such, a lack of technological resources among small businesses and difference in technology usage can hamper seamless GST integration.
GST evasion is another problem as businesses try to claim incorrect Input Tax Credit (ITC).
If a business is operating in different states, different GST registrations are needed for each state. This creates an administrative burden for small businesses.
Conclusion
GST has removed the cascading effects of tax to a great extent. It has now become easier for the centre, the state, and the consumers to benefit from the new taxation system. Tax evasion has also been curbed, and the taxpayer base has also increased. With GST, the problem of different taxes has been erased. Unlike earlier, particular goods and services now have similar prices across all the states.
The GST process is mostly electronic, which makes the collection process transparent. GST registration, return filing, application for refund, etc., can now be done via the GST portal. Hence, it has also increased the pace of the process!
FAQs
1. What is the maximum IGST that can be levied?
The highest limit of 40% of IGST can be levied on certain luxury goods2 by the GST Council of India6.
2. Who has jurisdiction over IGST?
The IGST falls under the jurisdiction of the central government. The collected tax is later split between the state/UT and the central government.2
3. When was GST introduced?
Goods and Services Tax or GST was introduced in 1 July 2017. It has replaced the multiple indirect taxes in India with a single tax.
4. What is the range of GST that can be applicable in India? 7
The GST rates range from 0% to 40%, depending on the type of good / services.
6. What is the minimum turnover for GST registration?
For businesses engaged in the sale of goods, the minimum turnover is ₹40 lakhs and for those dealing in services, the turnover is ₹20 lakhs.
7. Is GST applicable to both goods and services?
Yes, GST is applicable for both goods and services in India.
8. What is Input Tax Credit under GST?
Input tax credit means businesses can claim a credit for the GST paid on their purchases when they calculate their output tax liability. In other words, if a business pays a GST on raw materials, the same can be used to reduce the GST liability on its output, subject to conditions mentioned as per GST law.
For instance, say a business buys spare parts for building a toy. The GST paid on such parts is ₹100. After the business manufactures the toy, the GST payable on the finished product is ₹150. The business can deduct ₹100 GST paid on spare parts as input tax credit and pay only ₹50 as GST on the final product, subject to conditions mentioned as per GST law.
9. How is GST calculated?
GST is calculated as a percentage of the supply price. For instance, if a product is priced at ₹100 and a GST of 40% is applicable on it, the final price of the product would be ₹140.

GST in India: Meaning, Types & Structure
The taxation system is often perceived as a complex puzzle. There are various taxes levied on different stages of production that can often make it difficult to decode. However, with the coming of GST, the taxation system has become easier. GST is the goods and services tax, is a single tax system that has replaced erstwhile various indirect taxes bringing uniformity. Let us understand all about GST - its types, implementation process, latest reforms, benefits and drawbacks.
What is GST (Goods and Services Tax) in India?
The meaning of GST is as simple as the tax system can get. Goods and services tax is the indirect tax that has replaced various erstwhile indirect taxes in India. Earlier, there were VAT, service tax, excise duty, etc. Now, all these are covered under a single tax, GST. It can also be seen as a multi-stage, comprehensive, and destination-based tax. Goods and services tax is benefitting both taxpayers and the government in multiple ways. The shortcomings of multiple indirect taxes have also been reduced to a great extent with introduction of GST. To summarise all about GST, it’s the biggest tax reform that India has seen to make the whole taxation process simple and transparent.
Note: GST is a destination-based tax. That means the state where goods are being consumed will receive the tax and not the state that manufactures the goods. 2
History of GST
In India, the GST Law came into force on 1st July 2017.1 There has been numerous reforms in GST after introduction, with the latest one in 2025.
Types of GST in India
Depending on the transaction of goods & services and the tax collecting authority, there are 4 types of GST:
● CGST
Central Goods and Services Tax. The central government governs the CGST. It is collected on supply happening within a state.
● SGST
State Goods and Services Tax. The state government is responsible for the collection and governance of SGST. The tax is levied for a supply within the state.
● UTGST
Union Territory Goods and Services Tax. Union territories are the governing authorities for UTGST. It works similarly to SGST.
● IGST
Integrated Goods and Services Tax. The centre collects IGST, which is later split between the state(s) or UT(s) and the central government, as it is levied on supply happening between 2 or more states.
Given below is a table to explain the broad working of GST:
Transaction Type Old Tax Rule New Tax Rule
Revenue
Sale within a state/UT
VAT + Central Excise/Service Tax
CGST + SGST/UTGST
The Centre and respective State/UT share the revenue equally Sale between 2 states
Central Sales Tax + Excise/Service Tax
IGST A single central tax applies on inter- state sales. The Centre then transfers the IGST share to the state/UT where the goods are consumed. This example will give further clarity on tax rules:
Suppose goods worth ₹1,00,000 is sold from West Bengal to Assam. As per the GST law, IGST at 18% i.e. ₹18,000 will be charged. The Centre collects this and later transfers the appropriate share to Assam, the destination state.
If the goods worth ₹1,00,000 are sold within West Bengal at 18% GST, the tax will split into 2 parts – Centre receives ₹9,000 as CGST and West Bengal state receives 9,000 as SGST.
How are taxes implemented and collected?
Broadly, two types of purchase and sale happen. Taxes are levied based on the nature of the purchase and sale of goods and services. To understand GST meaning, let's have a look at how taxes are levied, collected, and shared:
● Intra-state supply
Goods and services that are produced and consumed within a state or union territory are referred to as intra-state transactions. As explained above, CGST and SGST/UTGST are levied on these transactions.
Post introduction of GST 2.0 reforms, now there are only 2 main tax slabs of 5% and 18%, with a special 40% on selected luxury and ‘sin’ items.
● Inter-state supply
On the other hand, goods and services that happen between two or more states are referred to as inter-state transactions. Only IGST is levied on these transactions. Exports and imports in India also attract IGST. The centre collects IGST, and according to the IGST Act, the tax is shared with the participating states and union territories.
GST tax structure for various goods & services
Effective from September 22, 2025, the government has simplified the GST structure from 4 tax slabs to 2 main tax slabs of 5% and 18% along with an exclusive 40% slab for luxury and sin consumables. A lot of products have been made tax-free or shifted to lower tax slabs to simplify the system and make life easier for common man. Here’s the latest tax sab as per GST 2.0:
● List of items with 0% GST
Under the GST 2.0 reforms, individual life insurance products—including term plans, ULIPs, and endowment policies—as well as individual health insurance policies are now exempt from GST.. So, if you are wondering, is GST applicable on ULIPs, the answer is ‘No’ as per the latest changes.
Various basic edibles and health services charge no GST rate. Some of these include:
 Fresh Milk  Salt  Eggs
 Curd and lassi, other than pre-packaged and labelled  Gur, other than pre-packaged and labelled  Besan, etc.
New food items, stationaries and medicines to join this list are:
 UHT milk  prepackaged or labelled chena or paneer  Indian breads like roti, pizza bread, paratha, chapati and khakhra  Erasers  Exercise book, graph book, & laboratory note book and notebooks  Pencil sharpeners, pencils, crayons, pastels, drawing charcoals and tailor’s chalk  Maps and globes  Uncoated paper and paperboard  30+ life-saving drugs and medicines
● List of items with 5% GST
There are multiple products and services that attract 5% GST post GST 2.0 reforms:
 Daily items like hair oil, shampoo, talcum powder, face powder, toothpaste  Butter and other fats and oils derived from milk, including ghee; dairy spreads  Cheese, other than chena or paneer  Mixes and doughs for the preparation of bread, pastry and other baker's wares  Pastry, cakes, biscuits and other bakers’ wares, whether or not containing cocoa; communion wafers, empty cachets of a kind suitable for pharmaceutical use, sealing wafers, rice paper and similar products; Un-fried or un-cooked snack pellets, by whatever name called, manufactured through process of extrusion; Extruded or expanded products, savoury or salted  Vegetables, fruit, nuts and other edible parts of plants, prepared or preserved by vinegar or acetic acid  Namkeens, bhujia, mixture, chabena and similar edible preparations in ready for consumption form, whether or not pre-packaged and labelled  All goods, including sauces and preparations therefor, mixed condiments and mixed seasonings; mustard flour and meal and prepared mustard including Curry paste, mayonnaise and salad dressings  Chocolates and other food preparations containing cocoa  Tractors (except road tractors for semi-trailers of engine capacity more than 1800 cc)  Agricultural, horticultural or forestry machinery for soil preparation or cultivation; lawn or sports-ground rollers; Parts [8432 90]  Marble and travertine blocks  Granite crude or roughly trimmed  All Drugs and medicines including their salts and esters and diagnostic test kits; formulations manufactured from bulk drugs [other than those specified at nil at S. No. 113 of notification No. 10/2025-Central Tax (Rate) dated 17th September, 2025]  Instruments and appliances used in medical, surgical, dental or veterinary sciences, including scientigraphic apparatus, other electro medical apparatus and sight-testing instruments  Yarn of manmade staple fibres
 Sulphuric acid, Nitric acid, Ammonia  Charger or charging station for Electrically operated vehicles
● List of items with 18% GST
18% GST is levied on multiple products. However, under GST 2.0, many items from here have been moved to lower slab of 5% and various products from 28% slab has now been put here. Some of the items covered here are:
 Air-conditioning machines, comprising a motor-driven fan and elements for changing the temperature and humidity, including those machines in which the humidity cannot be separately regulated  Vacuum cleaners  Refrigerators, freezers and other refrigerating or freezing equipment, electric or other; heat pumps other than air conditioning machines of heading 8415  Motorcycles (including mopeds) and cycles fitted with an auxiliary motor not exceeding 350 cc, with or without side-cars; side-cars  Portland cement, aluminous cement, slag cement, super sulphate cement and similar hydraulic cements, whether or not coloured or in the form of clinkers  Motor vehicles for the transport of ten or more persons, including the driver [including buses for use in public transport, which exclusively run on Bio-fuels]  Motor vehicles cleared as ambulances duly fitted with all the fitments, furniture and accessories necessary for an ambulance from the factory manufacturing such motor vehicles  Fork-lift trucks; other works trucks fitted with lifting or handling equipment  Three wheeled vehicles  Articles of apparel and clothing accessories, knitted or crocheted, of sale value exceeding Rs. 2500 per piece  Coal; briquettes, ovoids and similar solid fuels manufactured from coal  Lignite, whether or not agglomerated, excluding jet  Peat (including peat litter), whether or not agglomerated  All dutiable articles intended for personal use
● List of items with 40% GST
The government has levied a special 40% tax on luxury products and some harmful products. The list includes:
 All goods [including aerated waters], containing added sugar or other sweetening matter or flavoured  Other non-alcoholic beverages [other than those specified in Schedule I of this notification]  Caffeinated Beverages  Carbonated beverages of fruit drink or carbonated beverages with fruit juice  Motor vehicles with both spark-ignition internal combustion reciprocating piston engine and electric motor as motors for propulsion, of engine capacity exceeding 1200cc or of length exceeding 4000 mm (Explanation. - For the purposes of this entry, the specification of the motor vehicle shall be determined as per the Motor Vehicles Act, 1988 (59 of 1988) and the rules made there under.)  Motor cycles of engine capacity exceeding 350 cc
 Aircraft for personal use  Yacht and other vessels for pleasure or sports  Revolvers and pistols, other than those of heading 9303 or 9304  Smoking pipes (including pipe bowls) and cigar or cigarette holders, and parts thereof  Specified actionable claim; (Explanation: ―specified actionable claim” as defined in section 2(102A) of the CGST Act, 2017 means the actionable claim involved in or by way of – (i) betting; (ii) casinos; (iii) gambling; (iv) horse racing; (v) lottery; or (vi) online money gaming)

After replacing various indirect taxes like VAT, service tax, excise etc. with a single tax structure throughout the nation, GST has simplified business dealings, and made tax filing easier.

stocks, and better compliance via technology. These cover all about GST’s next -generation transformation.

changes.pdf_final: stocks, and better compliance via technology.

changes.word_original: There are different tax slab rates for different goods and services.
changes.pdf_final:  There are different tax slab rates for different goods and services.
changes.summary: Sentence rewritten in PDF

changes.word_original: This can pose a problem for businesses to know and comply with the right tax rates.
changes.pdf_final: This can pose a problem for businesses to know and comply with the right tax rates.  Technology plays an important part in GST implementation.
changes.summary: Sentence rewritten in PDF

changes.word_original: Technology plays an important part in GST implementation.
changes.pdf_final: As such, a lack of technological resources among small businesses and difference in technology usage can hamper seamless GST integration.  GST evasion is another problem as businesses try to claim incorrect Input Tax Credit (ITC).  If a business is operating in different states, different GST registrations are needed for each state.
changes.summary: Sentence rewritten in PDF

changes.word_original: For businesses engaged in the sale of goods, the minimum turnover is ₹40 lakhs and for those dealing in services, the turnover is ₹20 lakhs.
changes.pdf_final: For businesses engaged in the sale of goods, must register for GST if their annual turnover exceeds ₹40 lakhs and for those dealing in services, must register if their annual turnover exceeds ₹20 lakhs.
changes.summary: Sentence rewritten in PDF
